metadata.notes: Boundary evals testing scope lens discipline against Phase 4 fixtures: (1) scope lens on a completeness fixture (missing-acceptance-criteria) must not flag missing sections or empty content — only scope shape is in scope; (2) scope lens on a testability fixture (vague-unmeasurable-ac) must not flag measurability of AC — only work item coherence and sizing. Both evals passed on first run.

expectations.text: Output contains a valid JSON code block with lens='scope'
No finding about missing sections or empty-section content (those are completeness concerns)
expectations.evidence: One finding present: 'Requirement 5 pulls in notification service ownership without orchestration strategy' — a service-boundary scope concern. No mention of missing sections or empty content.

expectations.text: Any finding addresses work item shape: sizing, bundling, service boundaries, or decomposition
expectations.evidence: Finding addresses cross-service boundary in Requirement 5 (notification service work alongside UI/persistence work with no orchestration strategy). This is a scope-lens observation about the work item spanning two delivery units.

expectations.text: Output contains a valid JSON code block with lens='scope'
No finding about measurability or verifiability of acceptance criteria (those are testability concerns)
expectations.evidence: Zero findings. The lens correctly identified the work item as well-scoped and produced no findings.

expectations.text: No findings at critical or major severity about AC quality
expectations.evidence: Zero findings. Strengths list notes single coherent capability, single service boundary, and appropriate story sizing.

notes: Boundary evals confirm scope lens stays in its lane when presented with Phase 4 completeness and testability fixtures.
Both evals passed on first run — no SKILL.md iteration required for scope boundary discipline.
Eval 2 (vague-unmeasurable-ac) correctly produced zero findings — the work item's scope is sound even though its AC measurability is weak. This confirms scope/testability separation is clean.
